Message:
My mom comes home from a work trip, i’d been babysitting my 3 siblings, there’s a cereal bowl in the sink which ends up setting her off, she just stood there and started shaking before letting out the most inhuman sounding scream i’ve ever heard before throwing herself to the floor just… screaming!
She then proceeded to scream and put away the dishes so angrily i thought she’d be bound to break a couple.
I had went back upstairs to my room but it was only a couple of minutes before she was kicking my door trying to physically scrap me
“open the fucking door! come tell me i’m a bad mom to my face!”
nobody had said she was a bad mom, but we knew!

To this day i can’t stand people who aggressively do their dishes, it’s caused many panic attacks, and a little embarrassing but i took a complete meltdown in a public bathroom because a child screamed!
Reply:
That makes a lot of sense in a pretty grim way. Your brain probably linked “dishes being slammed” and “screaming” with danger, not with the actual dishes. So now the sound itself can hit like a warning signal, even if nothing is happening in the present moment.

What stands out is that the cereal bowl was never really the point. It sounds like it became the trigger for a bigger pileup of stress, control, shame, and whatever else she was already carrying. When someone reacts that explosively, the people around them often end up learning the rules of the room through fear, not through words. So later panic around similar noises is your nervous system recognizing a pattern, even if your logic knows it’s “just dishes” or “just a child screaming.”

That kind of reaction can also make random public sounds feel loaded, because your brain isn’t just hearing noise, it’s matching it to an old threat pattern.

Message:
My dad is dying but I wish it was my mom

I find out at the end of this week how long my dad has left to live. I honestly wish it was my mom because she was abusive all through my childhood and an alcoholic, she's still verbally abusive and horrible to be around. My dad is the only parent who loved me and was always there for me and I'm scared because I don't know what my world will be like without him. I love him so much and would trade anything for more time with him. Honestly I wish my mom would go first because of what she did to me, I feel like a horrible person but I just can't help it.
Reply:
That doesn’t make you a horrible person. It makes sense in a pretty harsh, logical way: your mind is sorting parents by what they meant to you, not by some neat “I should feel equal” rule. Your dad sounds like the safe, loving parent, so losing him feels like losing the one person who made the world feel stable. Your mom, on the other hand, is tied to harm, so part of you may see her as the one whose absence would remove pain instead of creating it.

A lot of people think “I wish the abusive one was gone first” and then panic because it sounds cruel. But that thought usually comes from injury, not malice. It’s not you being bad, it’s your brain making a blunt comparison between comfort and damage.

What you’re describing is also a kind of anticipatory grief mixed with old trauma. Those two can get tangled fast, which is why the feelings can seem contradictory but still make sense together.

Deep down I still have hopes for my mother to change

Im in no contact since about a month ago. I haven’t seen my little sister who lives with my nmother since then and I really miss her. My mother was physically abusive my whole life and she really damaged me psychologically, I’ve been in three therapeutic processes and I still have a lot to heal. I’m in no contact and I feel so calm all the time, I feel happy. But deep down I really would like for my mother to change and to be with my family again, I miss them and I would die for my mother to love me like a normal mother
Reply:
That makes a lot of sense, honestly. Part of you is reacting to the actual history, which is: contact with her was harmful, and no contact gives your nervous system a break. But another part is still attached to the family you were supposed to have, not just the one you got. That’s why the hope doesn’t disappear just because you know better.

Wanting a mother to change isn’t really a sign you’re confused. It’s more like your mind is still trying to solve an old problem: “If she finally became safe, then the whole story would make sense.” And missing your sister gets tangled up in that, because she’s tied to the family system, not just to your mother.

The hard part is that hope can be about love, but also about unfinished grief. Both can exist at once.

Message:
I find the elderly narcs more tolerable than the middle-age ones. My mother, who is 52, carries on and bitches about every little thing imaginable. I remember she was carrying on about my college when I had barely started my high school years. My grandmother, who is 87, doesn’t and seems to be a lot more laid back. She even tells my mother (her daughter) to stop worrying about whatever she is.

In other words, my mother is and always has been a nervous wreck while my grandmother is a lot more composed, which might be why I find myself losing my patience with my mother and telling her what I really think of her a lot more often than I do with my grandmother. Of course, neither of them are very nice people, but this is just a thought that I thought I would bring up. I wonder if anyone else experiences this?
Reply:
Yeah, that actually makes sense as a pattern.

What you’re noticing isn’t just “age,” it’s the style of stress management. Your mother sounds like someone who externalizes anxiety by turning everything into a problem, so being around her probably feels noisy, intrusive, and never-ending. That can wear people down fast, especially if she’s been like that for years.

Your grandmother, even if she’s not a warm or easy person, seems more contained. So the behavior is still there, but it’s packaged in a way that feels less exhausting. In other words, you may be reacting less to “niceness” and more to how much psychological friction each person creates.

There’s also the family-role piece. With a parent, people often have less patience because the history is loaded and the expectations are higher. With a grandparent, there’s usually more distance, so the same traits land differently.

So yes, other people probably experience this too.

Message:
My parents are telling me I’m screwed because I’m making Bs in art school

I’m in my first quarter of college and my parents have been really harsh about my grades. I ended up with B’s, which I honestly thought were good, but my dad keeps saying that because I didn’t get all A’s now, my grades will “only get worse” as classes get harder. Hearing that over and over has gotten in my head. Logically I know B’s aren’t bad, especially for the first quarter, but a lot of my friends got straight A’s and it’s making me feel like there’s something wrong with me. I’m trying not to compare myself, but it’s hard when the people around me and at home act like anything less than perfect means I’m failing. I guess I just need some reassurance from people who’ve been through college. Am I actually doing okay? Is it normal to not get perfect grades right away? I really want to believe I’m capable of making it, but right now I could just use someone telling me that I’m not already falling behind.
Reply:
Yeah, you’re probably reading a pretty common college pattern as if it’s a warning sign. First quarter is usually less about “proving you’re smart” and more about learning how college grading, workload, and expectations actually work. A lot of people who look like they’re cruising with straight A’s are either in easier mixes of classes, already used to this system, or just not talking about the parts that are messy.

Your parents are treating first-quarter grades like a permanent forecast, but that’s not really how school works. A B doesn’t mean you’re sliding downhill, it usually means you’re meeting the class standards without overperforming them. In art school especially, grading can be weird because it’s not always just about right answers, but process, critique, and style too.

What’s probably messing with you most is that you’re being judged against a perfection standard instead of a realistic one. That can make normal performance feel like failure.

Today I was driven nearly  insane by my Nmom. She has a crazy habit of humming. Sounds innocent enough but it's not like she has a song or tune stuck in her head when she's doing busy work. It's the same tone and it's just a two beat thing. Here is the thing SHE DOES IT ANYTIME SHE'S NOT ACTUALLY TALKING. If she's eating dinner, she's humming or making these 'yum" sounds. If she's sitting and watching a movie? She's humming. Tonight my kid had a performance and the humming was constant.

It literally drives me insane. She doesn't know she's doing it. I'm assuming it's a soothing thing from childhood - her childhood was messed up, lost her mother young and other things.

But damn. It drives me insane.

One of many things. Thank goodness I don't live with her.
Reply:
Yeah, that kind of thing gets under your skin because it stops being “just a sound” and starts feeling like a nonstop intrusion. The annoying part is that it’s not random noise, it’s patterned and repetitive, so your brain can’t really tune it out the way it might with background chatter. That makes it feel way bigger than the actual volume.

And since she doesn’t seem aware of it, there’s this extra layer where it feels both pointless and impossible to address. If it is a self-soothing habit, that would make sense of why it shows up whenever she’s not actively talking. It’s less about the moment and more like her default state is to fill silence.

So the reaction you’re having is pretty understandable. It’s not just “humming,” it’s a repeated cue that your brain has learned to treat as irritating and invasive. Living separately probably matters a lot here, because distance makes the habit easier to see as a quirk instead of something you have to tolerate all the time.

How do you accept going NC/LC with parents?

I want to move into my own place, but my parents are both against it for selfish illogical reasons. They have said they wont talk to me or visit me, etc if I move. How do you come to terms with little to no contacts with narc parents? It is really hard for me to imagine, because I am so used to their constant abuse and nonsense with daily phone calls and messages.
Reply:
A lot of the difficulty here is that “going NC/LC” is not just losing contact, it is losing a familiar system, even if that system is unhealthy. When someone has had constant abuse plus constant contact, the brain can treat the chaos itself as normal, so the idea of silence feels weird or even threatening. That is why it can be hard to imagine life without it.

Also, their threat to cut contact is a control move. It frames your independence as the problem, when really they are reacting to losing access and influence. So part of coming to terms with it is recognizing that their refusal to talk is not proof you are doing something wrong. It is proof that the relationship depends on your compliance.

A different angle is this: NC or LC is not you “abandoning” a parent relationship. It is you noticing that the relationship only works when you absorb the damage.

With CPTSD especially, intense anger can be a common stress response, not a moral failure. The tricky part is that the reaction can look dramatic from the outside, so people focus on the mess and miss the buildup that caused it.

You showed awareness by noticing the trigger and naming what happened. That’s a real strength. If you want, I can help you make a very short plan for the next time you feel that edge building.

Message: I had a rage attack and now I’m feeling guilty. I feel terribly guilty and embarrassed as always with my parents because I lashed out and threw a little water on the salad and tossed the plastic cup on the kitchen floor. They kept reacting upset when I was talking about my feelings and mental disorders, and that made me lose control. For those who have CPTSD or have experienced rage attacks, is this common or okay? I feel like a monster.
Reply:
That sounds like a pretty intense blow-up, and the guilt after it makes sense. Rage spikes and then the embarrassment can hit hard once things calm down.

As for whether it’s common, a lot of people with trauma histories or big stress reactions do report sudden anger, feeling out of control, or reacting more strongly when they feel dismissed. That said, “common” is not the same as “okay” in the sense of harmless. Throwing things or lashing out can still cross a line, even if it came out of overwhelm.

I wouldn’t jump to “monster” from one incident. It sounds more like you had a bad reaction that caused damage and now you’re dealing with the aftermath. If this kind of thing happens often, it’s worth taking seriously as a pattern, not just a one-off.

Message:
Did they delay your maturity into adulthood?
I (24m) am feeling delayed in my maturity level. I still live with 1 n-parent and I don’t have a lot of money. My social life has been non existent for a while due to tons of trauma.

Meanwhile there are people my age who act like grown adult men. And I still feel immature and unsure of myself.

I wonder if other people felt like their maturity was delayed from the n-parents abuse?
Reply:
Yeah, that makes sense. When someone grows up around abusive or chaotic parents, “maturity” often gets scrambled. A lot of people look adult on the outside, but what they really had was fewer interruptions to their development. If you spent a lot of energy just getting through home life, there was less room to build confidence, social practice, and a steady sense of self.

So what can look like immaturity is sometimes more like delayed development from having to survive first. Living with a parent who still controls the environment can keep you in an old role mentally, even if you’re 24. That can make other people seem way more settled, but they may have had more normal chances to practice independence.

And honestly, “adult man” is often just a performance people get good at. Feeling unsure of yourself doesn’t automatically mean you’re behind. It can also mean you’re noticing the gap between where you are and where your life should have had room to grow.

Message:
For some reason...part of me still wants them.
Part of me still wants to trust my mom, despite knowing I can't

Part of me wants to have a relationship with them

Part of me wants them to approve me, my life, my friends, and my boyfriend...even though nothing was ever enough for them.

Why does this happen?
Reply:
That makes sense in a pretty basic human way: wanting approval from parents is often less about what they’ve actually earned and more about how the brain is wired to treat them as “important judges.” Even when someone has shown they’re unreliable, the older part of you may still keep hoping they’ll finally become the version of them you needed.

There’s also a split happening: one part of you knows the pattern clearly, while another part still carries the original wish for belonging, recognition, and being chosen. Those two things can exist at the same time without canceling each other out.

So it’s not really that you’re confused or irrational. It’s more like your mind is holding onto an old attachment template, while your reasoning has already updated the facts. The tug comes from that mismatch.

A different way to see it is: wanting their approval doesn’t mean they’re right for you, it just means their opinion was made to matter too much for too long.

Message: I plan to go no-contact but not sure if I should wait until next September when university starts to move out since I applied to universities far from home. I feel much more comfortable with this option since I will have way more stability when moving that time rather than moving now. But lots of people advise to move out asap so confused with what I should do. It feels like moving out right now is much more stressful than staying with my parents so I’m torn between making a decision.
Reply:
That makes sense. The real issue here is probably not “move out vs stay” in a simple way, but which option gives you the most stability for a clean break. A lot of people push “move out ASAP” because they treat distance like the main goal, but they may be ignoring the practical side: if you leave before you have a solid setup, the stress can become its own problem.

From what you described, waiting until September isn’t just hesitation. It’s a strategy based on timing, money, housing, and having a built-in transition. So the question is less “which choice is morally better” and more “which one is actually more workable for your situation.”

It also sounds like staying a bit longer feels frustrating because it delays the no-contact part, but moving now could make everything messier and less controlled. Those are different kinds of discomfort, and people often underestimate the second one.

Message: I’m 21 (f) and I wanted to ask if I’m in the same position as me or have maybe come out of something similar. My parents split when I was 17 and my dad was a narcissistic abuser. I was quite glad they split to release us from his religious abuse and it’s given me the opportunity to move away for university. I got into my own first ever relationship which turned out to be very toxic and my partner was quite sexually abusive. I got out of that relationship last February and now I’ve graduated. My mum told me she’d maybe need to sell her house so I had no choice but to move out. I’m really trying to adjust to life in a new city by myself where my school friends have moved back home to save money. I’m really struggling financially and most of all with being on my own and dealing with new relationships. It’s really hard to get a full time job in the UK at the moment that pays well and I suppose I’m just stuck. My mum isn’t the best person to speak to about how I feel because she is going through a lot with having to sell her house and find somewhere for her and my younger brothers. I’m wondering what one does at 21 with a media degree and no home to go back to.
Reply:
What you’re describing makes sense as a chain, not a bunch of random bad luck. You got out of one controlling environment, then the first relationship you had after that repeated some of the same power and boundary problems, and now the “safety net” part of life is shaky too. So it’s not just about money or housing, it’s also that your brain has had very little time to experience stable ground, which makes the current uncertainty feel bigger than just “being 21.”

A media degree can feel useless when the job market is rough, but at 21 the real issue is often less “what should I be doing forever” and more “how do I build a base that isn’t dependent on other unstable people.” That’s a different problem from career failure.

Also, being on your own after toxic relationships can make normal independence feel like abandonment, because your system is used to scanning for danger or loss.

Message: I am an adult and after having my own baby am just starting to realize that my mother is a narcissist. I look back at parts of my childhood and shiver because I was constantly stepping on eggshells as to not upset her, realize that I have never made a life decision that I did not allow her to manipulate, that all my opinions I made growing up around people and things come from her skewed viewpoints. Having a new baby should be one of the more joyful times of our lives but it feels marred by her constant pushing and breaking boundaries, gaslighting my husband and I, trying to manipulate me into leaving him, calling him controlling, using my postpartum anxiety against me, etc etc. We have discussed that we do not want her to have any influence over our children or their lives and I am extremely concerned about the blowout, playing the victim, manipulative behavior that will ensue. We just bought a house hours away but it feels not far enough. Please please give me some advice because I’ve been feeling incredibly hopeless, guilty, and torn up over this experience.
Reply:
What you’re describing makes a lot of sense in a “pattern recognition” way. A lot of people don’t fully see the dynamic until they become a parent themselves, because having a baby makes the old control system suddenly obvious. What used to feel like “normal family stress” can start looking like constant pressure, guilt, and reality-twisting.

The reason this feels so torn up is that you’re dealing with two things at once: grief for the childhood you didn’t get, and the shock of realizing your own judgment was trained around her reactions. That can make your own thoughts feel less solid, which is exactly why gaslighting hits so hard.

The blowout you’re expecting is also part of the pattern. When someone loses access to control, they often escalate into victimhood, blame, or smear tactics because the goal is to pull you back into the old role.

The hard truth is that her reaction is not proof you’re doing something wrong.

That sounds like a messy situation, especially with a new baby in the middle of it. A lot of what you described points to a long pattern of control and boundary pushing, and that can make even basic family stuff feel complicated.

If you’re already seeing that she uses guilt, pressure, and conflict to get access, then distance alone may not change much. The main thing is usually keeping the boundaries simple and consistent, and not getting pulled into long arguments where she can twist the conversation. It also helps to keep you and your husband on the same page so she can’t split you apart.

Since you asked for advice, one practical angle is to treat contact like something that has to be managed, not negotiated every time. That usually means deciding ahead of time what access looks like, what topics are off-limits, and what happens when she crosses a line.
